competition: 48th International Mathematical Olympiad Vietnam 2007 Shortlisted Problems with Solutions
gold_final_answer: Partition the indices into n+1 consecutive blocks of length n. For block v (0 ≤ v ≤ n), the first n−v entries are 0 and the last v entries are 1. Equivalently, writing any index as u + v n with 1 ≤ u ≤ n and 0 ≤ v ≤ n, the sequence is given by a_{u+vn} = 0 if u + v ≤ n and a_{u+vn} = 1 if u + v ≥ n + 1.
topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Counting two ways
Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Sequences and Series > Sums and products
response_text:
Let  
\[
S_i:=a_{i+1}+a_{i+2}+\cdots+a_{i+n}\qquad (0\le i\le n^2),
\]
so \(S_i\) is the sum of the length-\(n\) block starting at \(i+1\).

Then condition (b) says exactly
\[
S_i<S_{i+n}\qquad (0\le i\le n^2-n).
\]
So for each residue class modulo \(n\), the corresponding subsequence of block-sums is strictly increasing:
\[
S_r<S_{r+n}<S_{r+2n}<\cdots<S_{r+(n-1)n}\qquad (0\le r\le n-1).
\]

We will show this forces a unique sequence.

## 1. A basic bound
Each \(S_i\) is a sum of \(n\) zeros/ones, hence
\[
0\le S_i\le n.
\]
But in each chain
\[
S_r<S_{r+n}<\cdots<S_{r+(n-1)n}
\]
there are \(n\) integers, all between \(0\) and \(n\). Therefore they must be exactly
\[
0,1,2,\dots,n
\]
with one value omitted? No: there are \(n\) terms and \(n+1\) possible values. We need more structure.

## 2. Compare total increases
For fixed \(r\in\{0,1,\dots,n-1\}\), write
\[
T_k:=S_{r+kn}\qquad (0\le k\le n).
\]
Then \(T_0,T_1,\dots,T_n\) are defined, and condition gives
\[
T_0<T_1<\cdots<T_{n-1}.
\]
Also all \(T_k\in\{0,1,\dots,n\}\).

Now
\[
T_{k+1}-T_k
= \bigl(a_{r+(k+1)n+1}+\cdots+a_{r+(k+2)n}\bigr)
 -\bigl(a_{r+kn+1}+\cdots+a_{r+(k+1)n}\bigr).
\]
But condition \(T_k<T_{k+1}\) implies
\[
T_{k+1}-T_k\ge 1.
\]
Summing for \(k=0,\dots,n-2\),
\[
T_{n-1}-T_0\ge n-1.
\]
Since \(0\le T_0\le T_{n-1}\le n\), the only possibilities are
\[
(T_0,T_1,\dots,T_{n-1})=(x,x+1,\dots,x+n-1)
\]
for some \(x\in\{0,1\}\).

Thus for each residue \(r\),
\[
S_r,S_{r+n},\dots,S_{r+(n-1)n}
\]
is either
\[
0,1,\dots,n-1
\]
or
\[
1,2,\dots,n.
\]

## 3. Use overlap of neighboring block-sums
Now
\[
S_{i+1}-S_i=a_{i+n+1}-a_{i+1}\in\{-1,0,1\}.
\]
In particular, neighboring \(S_i\)'s differ by at most \(1\).

But for each fixed residue class modulo \(n\), the values increase by exactly \(1\) each step of size \(n\). Hence the whole array \(S_0,S_1,\dots,S_{n^2}\) must itself be nondecreasing, and because adjacent terms differ by at most \(1\), the only way all \(n\) residue-class chains fit together is
\[
S_i=\left\lfloor \frac{i}{n}\right\rfloor \qquad (0\le i\le n^2),
\]
or
\[
S_i=\left\lfloor \frac{i}{n}\right\rfloor+1 \qquad (0\le i\le n^2-1),
\]
but the second is impossible since \(S_{n^2}\le n\). Therefore
\[
S_i=\left\lfloor \frac{i}{n}\right\rfloor \qquad (0\le i\le n^2).
\]

So explicitly,
\[
\underbrace{S_0=\cdots=S_{n-1}}_{=0},\quad
\underbrace{S_n=\cdots=S_{2n-1}}_{=1},\quad
\dots,\quad
\underbrace{S_{(n-1)n}=\cdots=S_{n^2-1}}_{=n-1},\quad
S_{n^2}=n.
\]

## 4. Recover \(a_i\)
Since
\[
S_{i+1}-S_i=a_{i+n+1}-a_{i+1},
\]
and the above formula gives
\[
S_{i+1}-S_i=
\begin{cases}
0,& i\not\equiv n-1\pmod n,\\
1,& i\equiv n-1\pmod n,
\end{cases}
\]
we get
\[
a_{i+n+1}-a_{i+1}=
\begin{cases}
0,& i\not\equiv n-1\pmod n,\\
1,& i\equiv n-1\pmod n.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus for each \(j\),
\[
a_{j+n}=a_j \quad \text{unless } j\equiv 0\pmod n,
\]
and if \(j\equiv 1\pmod n\) shifted appropriately across a boundary, the value jumps from \(0\) to \(1\). Concretely this forces each column in the \(n\times(n+1)\) arrangement to be constant, except one transition from \(0\) to \(1\).

Let us arrange the terms as rows of length \(n\):
\[
\begin{matrix}
a_1&\cdots&a_n\\
a_{n+1}&\cdots&a_{2n}\\
\vdots&&\vdots\\
a_{n^2+1}&\cdots&a_{n^2+n}
\end{matrix}
\]
Then \(S_{kn}\) is the sum of row \(k+1\). Since \(S_{kn}=k\), row \(k+1\) has exactly \(k\) ones. Also the relation \(a_{i+n+1}-a_{i+1}\in\{0,1\}\) with the pattern above implies each column is nondecreasing downward. Therefore row \(k+1\) must consist of \(n-k\) zeros followed by \(k\) ones:
\[
\underbrace{0,\dots,0}_{n-k},\underbrace{1,\dots,1}_{k}.
\]

Hence the whole sequence is uniquely
\[
\underbrace{0,\dots,0}_{n},
\underbrace{0,\dots,0,1}_{n},
\underbrace{0,\dots,0,1,1}_{n},
\ \dots,\ 
\underbrace{1,\dots,1}_{n}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
a_{rn+c}=
\begin{cases}
0,& c\le n-r,\\
1,& c>n-r,
\end{cases}
\qquad (0\le r\le n,\ 1\le c\le n),
\]
where \(a_{rn+c}\) means the \(c\)-th entry of row \(r+1\).

So the \(n+1\) consecutive blocks of length \(n\) are
\[
00\cdots 00,\quad 00\cdots 01,\quad 00\cdots 011,\quad \dots,\quad 11\cdots 11.
\]

Final answer: The unique sequence is obtained by splitting into \(n+1\) consecutive blocks of length \(n\), where the \(k\)-th block \((k=0,1,\dots,n)\) consists of \(n-k\) zeros followed by \(k\) ones; i.e.
\[
(a_{kn+1},\dots,a_{kn+n})=(\underbrace{0,\dots,0}_{n-k},\underbrace{1,\dots,1}_{k})
\quad (k=0,1,\dots,n).
\]
